Here's to courage, clarity, good luck, and lots more tea, Galloper of Good Fortune --- Field Notes - Leaf: 1 bag (or 1 tsp loose) per cup - Water: full boil, 100 °C / 212 °F - Steep: 4–5 minutes - Finish: plain or with a little milk; it is a festive spiced black, take it how the year finds you Acquisition - Direct from Harney & Sons (a seasonal release; it appears around Lunar New Year) - The exact tin: Harney & Sons Lunar New Year Tea

The Read Everyone has a type, even if youve never noticed it. The one with no story, the quiet mysterious stranger, brooding in a corner in a crowded bar. The one you konw you dont have to be interesting for, just giving yourself over to in the span of a whispered hello. No conversation held between the silence, only a glance, a fleeting moment of understanding. It was always this, it will always be this and nothing and no one can ever drag you away. The kettle ticks like something cooling that used to be alive. The incessant climbing and grating voice of atoms heating in a vaccum. You tell yourself you're choosing, standing there at the counter, but your hand has already gone where it always goes, past the pretty tins with their unbroken seals, to the box gone soft at the corners from a thousand openings. The cardboard remembers your grip better than you do. Steam stands up off the water into the heavy kitchen air and won't disperse, the way August won't, the way the smell reaches the back of your throat before you've lifted the cup. You have administered this to yourself ten thousand evenings and called it nothing, called it just tea, the way you call all your oldest hungers nothing. It was Ceylon the whole time. It was Ceylon inside everything you ever loved and never examined. The recognition arrives the way they always do down here: too late, and from inside the house, wearing the face of something you should have known was kin. --- The Letter [!dispatch] A Year of Denial and the Tea That Exposed Me Dear Esteemed Members of the BAMS Tea Club, We have officially completed one full year of this beautifully unhinged experiment, and to celebrate entering Year Two, I owe you all a confession. I have a problem. It started exactly one year ago this March, when I discovered Kirin Straight Tea. I thought it would be a brief seasonal fixation. The kind of thing I'd enjoy for a few weeks and then quietly retire, like every other hyperfixation that has ever entered my life. Reader. It has been twelve months. I am not better. I am, in fact, worse. I have not stopped. There is no sign of stopping. At this point, Kirin Straight Tea is less of a preference and more of a personality trait I have simply decided to adopt. So naturally, being the deeply self-aware and well-adjusted individual that I am, I eventually asked the important question: What is actually IN this tea? Ceylon Tea. It's Ceylon Tea. And then, because the universe apparently wanted to make a point, I looked up another longtime favorite — one I'd been drinking and enjoying for years without ever really thinking about it — and what do you know. Also Ceylon Tea. It turns out I have a type. I have always had a type. I was just too busy not paying attention to notice. Tea of the Month: Taylors of Harrogate Ceylon Tea This month, we're going to the source. Taylors of Harrogate Ceylon is a bright, clean, beautifully balanced black tea. Ceylon tea comes from Sri Lanka (formerly Ceylon), and it's one of the most widely loved black teas in the world — which means a lot of us have been drinking it for years without realizing it. It's the base of countless blends. It's the backbone of your favorite bottled tea. It is, apparently, the tea I keep returning to without ever consciously choosing it. The lesson? Sometimes you don't discover what you love — you just eventually notice that you've loved it all along. Or, more accurately: sometimes it takes a Japanese canned tea and an embarrassing consumption rate to teach you something about yourself. Either way, here we are. So steep it hot, sip it slow, and take a moment to ask yourself what your Ceylon Tea is. The thing you keep coming back to. The comfort you never had to explain. The hyperfixation that turned out to just be… you. Welcome to Year Two. This ride only gets more specific from here. --- Field Notes - Leaf: 1 bag (or 1 tsp loose) per cup - Water: full boil, 100 °C / 212 °F. Ceylon black wants the heat - Steep: 3–5 minutes; it stays bright and rarely turns bitter - Finish: clean enough to drink plain, but takes milk without losing its edge Acquisition - Direct from Taylors of Harrogate , or any decent grocer. Ceylon is the backbone of countless blends, so it is nearly everywhere - The exact tin: Taylors of Harrogate Pure Ceylon

The Read Some people can only say the enormous thing sideways. Said straight it would be too much, would crack something open neither of you is ready for, so they reach for a joke instead, the more absurd the better, and hand it to you grinning, and the joke is a container. It has to be ridiculous enough to carry the weight without either of you having to look directly at it. A scrotum-shaped tea bag is, against all odds, a perfect vessel for I love you . The stupidity is load-bearing. The stupidity is how he keeps from saying it too plainly and meaning it too much. So you brew the absurd thing, you watch it dip, you reckon fully with what is happening in the mug, and you laugh, because you are meant to. And then near the bottom, with no joke left to hide behind, he sets it down plain: I saw this product and thought of all of you immediately. This is what I think of when I think of the people I care about most. He hands you a gag and a naked declaration in the same breath, signs it Earl of Questionable Judgment , and trusts you to know which part he means. Both. He always means both. That is the whole architecture of how some people love you: the bit is real, the warmth underneath it is realer, and the bit exists so the warmth has somewhere safe to live. --- The Letter [!dispatch] No, This Is Not an April Fool's Joke. I Wish I Were That Creative Dear Members of the BAMS Tea Club, April Fool's has come and gone. This is not a prank. I need you to understand that before you keep reading, because what I am about to tell you is going to sound like one. This month's tea is from a company called Sack'd. Their bags are designed to look like little nutsacks. Every cup you brew is, technically and intentionally, tea bagging. The company is aware of this. They built their entire brand around it. I have never respected a small business more in my life, and frankly I am a little emotional about it. Tea of the Month: Earl Grey by Sack'd It's an Earl Grey, which is a classic, which means the tea itself is almost certainly fine. Totally normal. Very sophisticated. Just you, alone in your kitchen, tea bagging with complete dignity. What I cannot prepare you for is the experience of making it. The brewing process. The dipping. The moment you look down at your mug and fully reckon with what is happening in there. That is something you need to have for yourself, and I say that with the utmost respect for each and every one of you. I saw this product and thought of all of you immediately. I want you to sit with that. Really let it sink in. This is what I think of when I think of the people I care about most. You're welcome. Steeped in chaos and zero regrets, Earl of Questionable Judgment --- Field Notes - Leaf: 1 bag (or 1 tsp) per cup - Water: just off the boil, 96 °C / 205 °F - Steep: 3–4 minutes; pull it before the bergamot turns bitter - Finish: takes milk well; a thin round of lemon if you're feeling English about it Acquisition - Direct from Sack'd : wink-house.com - Standard grocery-aisle Earl Grey is a fine stand-in for the tea; the bag is the bit.

The Read Anything can happen once. Once is an accident, a story you tell later with a shrug, a thing that visited and left no forwarding address. It's the second time that damns you. The second time is a pattern admitting what it is, a hand you didn't know you'd been holding until it tightened. Nobody asks to be in the habit of someone. You just look up one ordinary morning and find the thing has installed itself behind your day, load-bearing, and that the day would tilt without it now. It comes bold and malty and unreasonably strong, the kind of black that stains the cup and means business before you do. You take it with milk, with sugar, with a splash of something browner if the morning has already gone wrong. This is the tea with no delicacy in it and no apology, steeped dark enough to stand a spoon in, drunk fast and too hot in the gray hour when the house is still asleep and it is only you and the kettle and the thing you are not yet ready to face. It holds the day up while you find your feet. And by the time the cup is empty you are, against your will and without ceremony, a person who does this now. --- The Letter [!dispatch] We're Still Doing This Dear Unquestionably Devoted BAMS Tea Club Member, Guess what? We're still doing this. That's right—what started as an impulsive act of friendship-fueled chaos has somehow become an actual recurring event (is twice considered recurring?). So, welcome back to another month of tea you didn't ask for but are now obligated to drink out of sheer curiosity. In honor of St. Patrick's Day, I present to you this month's selection! Tea of the Month: Irish Breakfast Tea by Taylors of Harrogate This is a bold, malty, highly caffeinated black tea that is best enjoyed with milk, sugar, or—if you're feeling extra Irish—a splash of whiskey. (I'm not saying you should… but I'm also not saying you shouldn't.) BAMS Tea Club Goes Digital! That's right, we now have an official (but still deeply unserious) website: https://bamstea.club Here, you can: ✔ Review this month's tea (because your opinion matters… probably). ✔ Revisit past teas (to either relive the magic or remember what to avoid). ✔ Validate my impulsive decision-making (or, you know, just see how deep the tea rabbit hole goes). What's Next? The BAMS Tea Club train of chaos rolls on, and who knows what next month will bring? (Spoiler: me. I know. But you don't. Ha.) Until then, enjoy your tea, embrace the madness, and may the luck of the Irish keep your mug full. Sláinte! --- Field Notes - Leaf: 1 bag (or 1 tsp loose) per cup - Water: full boil, 100 °C / 212 °F - Steep: 4–5 minutes; it can take it - Finish: milk and sugar, and a splash of whiskey if the morning earned it Acquisition - Direct from Taylors of Harrogate , or any grocer with a tea aisle - The exact box: Taylors of Harrogate Irish Breakfast

The Read There is a kind of bravery in letting yourself have the soft thing. The strong stuff is easy. The bold, the bitter, the punch you can hide behind, the cup that doubles as a shield. It is harder to admit you wanted something delicate, citrus-bright, barely sweet, the kind of cup that does not armor you against the day but opens a window in it. Spring asks this of you every year, and every year you resist, and every year it wins anyway, because the body gets tired of bracing and remembers, somewhere old and unguarded, what it was to be tended instead of defended. It arrives in a silk sachet like it already knows it is better than the dust you came up on, whole leaves you can see through the mesh, a pale gold steep that smells like the first afternoon warm enough to leave a window open. Grapefruit on the front of it, vanilla underneath, nothing in the cup heavy enough to call comfort and all of it doing comfort's quieter work. You drink it slow because it asks to be drunk slow, and something in the chest lets go a degree it had forgotten it was holding. Fresh starts are mostly a story we sell ourselves every April. This one, for the length of a cup, almost manages to be true. --- The Letter [!dispatch] Spring Has Sprung, and So Has Our Taste Level Dear Floral-Infused Friends of the Leaf, Welcome to April, aka that time of year when we all collectively pretend we enjoy being outside. Birds are chirping, bees are buzzing, and seasonal allergies are attacking—but we don't care, because we've got tea. And not just any tea. Oh no. This month, we're getting a little bougie. Tea of the Month: White Vanilla Grapefruit by Harney & Sons Take a deep breath, because this month's selection is an experience. We're stepping away from the punch-you-in-the-face strength of black teas and gliding into something lighter, brighter, and just a little fancier. Say hello to White Vanilla Grapefruit by Harney & Sons. This is our very first white tea, and yes, that's a thing. It's delicate, slightly sweet, and kissed with a hint of citrus and vanilla. But let's talk packaging—because we're officially moving out of the "dust in a paper bag" phase of our lives. These come in luxurious silk sachets filled with actual whole tea leaves like we're royalty or something. It smells like spring. It tastes like fresh starts. And it brews like it knows it's better than you. Until next month, may your mugs be warm, your allergies mild, and your tea leaves whole. Blooming with chaotic affection, Supreme Steeper of the Leaves --- Field Notes - Leaf: 1 silk sachet (or 1 tsp loose) per cup - Water: below the boil, 80 °C / 175 °F; white tea scorches if you bully it - Steep: 2–3 minutes, gently - Finish: plain. It is too delicate to survive milk Acquisition - Direct from Harney & Sons - The exact sachets: Harney & Sons White Vanilla Grapefruit

The Read Everyone expected something virtuous. Something floral and forgivable, a tea you could drink in front of your better self. You went the other way. There is a particular pleasure in the swerve, in reaching past the wholesome thing for the one that tastes like getting away with something, the dessert you did not earn and are going to have anyway. Wanting was never the sin they raised you to flinch from, whatever they told you. Some nights the most honest thing in the house is the craving you finally stop apologizing for. It steeps up dark and rich, cocoa nibs lending the black a low sweetness that settles on the tongue like a confession you are glad you finally made. Cake in a cup, the letter says, and it is not wrong, but it is more than that. It is permission in liquid form, the warm indulgent dark you let yourself sink into at the end of a day that took more than it returned. You measure the loose leaf out slow, like an apothecary dosing something medicinal, because in the ways that matter it is. You add the milk. You add the sugar. You are allowed this. That was always, underneath all the chaos and the jokes about manatee diffusers, the entire point of the operation. --- The Letter [!dispatch] A Hard Left Turn into the Dessert Cart Dear Faithful Disciples of the Steep, You probably thought May's tea would be something floral, didn't you? Like, "Ooooh it's spring! Let's do lavender-chamomile with dried rose petals and name it after a woodland fairy." No. Absolutely not. This month, we're throwing a hard left turn right into the dessert cart. Because nothing says "celebrate the season of renewal" quite like Chocolate Black Tea. Yes, you read that right. It's chocolate. In tea. Specifically: a rich, full-bodied black tea blended with cocoa nibs and chocolate flavoring that makes every sip feel like you're having cake in liquid form—but make it cozy and adult-ish. This is a loose leaf tea, which means you're now part of the fancy tea club that measures leaves with teaspoons like an actual apothecary. I've included disposable tea bags for your convenience (because I love you), but if you're the kind of person who owns a tea diffuser shaped like a manatee, by all means, live your truth. Recommended brew: - One teaspoon per cup - Steep 5 minutes at just-boiled temperature - Add milk and sugar if you're feeling extra indulgent (you are) May your tea be rich, your diffuser not leak, and your chocolate cravings be momentarily satisfied. Forever your cocoa-concocting companion, Grand Poobah of Piping Hot Sips --- Field Notes - Leaf: 1 tsp loose per cup - Water: just off the boil, 95 °C / 205 °F - Steep: 5 minutes - Finish: milk and sugar. It is dessert; lean in Acquisition - Direct from Harney & Sons - The exact tin: Harney & Sons Chocolate Black

The Read Ten years is long enough that the vows stop being a promise and turn into a fact, a thing you live inside instead of recite. Nobody warns you that the romance of it goes quiet and becomes something stranger and better: a shared archive, a decade of small mornings, a person who knows where the wedding box is kept and what is in it and why you have never once thrown out the handkerchief. Love that lasts does not look like the wedding. It looks like still being here, still steeping the same leaf, a decade deep into the long ordinary miracle of not having left. So he raided her memory box like a sentimental thief and mailed you the evidence: the same delicate white tea they handed out the day they married, rosebuds and lemon and vanilla, sweet the way only a thing soaked in ten years of meaning gets to be. A wedding wand waved once, joyously, then shut in the dark for a decade. A handkerchief old enough to have caught someone else's happy tears first. A paper fan thick as a vow. You steep the tea and for the length of a cup you are a guest at a wedding you never attended, inside a marriage that kept going long after the music stopped, which is the only part that was ever really the point. --- The Letter [!dispatch] A Wedding You Didn't Attend Dear Distant Guests of a Wedding You Didn't Attend, This month's selection is less "surprise tea roulette" and more sentimental flashback, so bear with me while I get a little nostalgic (but not like, feelings-nostalgic—we're not monsters). This month's tea isn't just a nod to the past—it is the past. June marks 10 years since your beloved Tea Master got hitched. That's a full decade of marriage, mugs, mutual weirdness, and an alarming number of shared Amazon Prime orders. In honor of that milestone, I'm sending you the same tea we gave out as favors at our wedding—along with something a little extra. Tea of the Month: Harney & Sons Wedding Tea A delicate blend of mutan white tea with rosebuds, petals, lemon, and vanilla, it's sweet, floral, and weirdly romantic without trying too hard—basically the tea equivalent of: "Yes, I'll marry you, but only if we can have a cheese bar at the reception." But that's not all. We're taking things one step further this month, because I raided my wife's wedding memory box like a very sentimental pirate, and inside were treasures that hadn't seen the light of day in a decade—until now. In addition to your tea, you'll find a few original keepsakes from our big day: An Irish wedding wand – Waved joyously during the recessional. Equal parts festive and fabulous. An antique handkerchief – For catching happy tears, wiping scone crumbs, or just feeling classy. A wedding program fan – Printed on cardstock thick enough to serve as both literature and personal cooling system. Minister of Matrimonial Infusions --- Field Notes - Leaf: 1 sachet (or 1 tsp loose) per cup - Water: below the boil, 80 °C / 175 °F; it is a white blend, treat it gently - Steep: 2–3 minutes - Finish: plain, so the rosebuds and vanilla carry Acquisition - Direct from Harney & Sons - The exact blend: Harney & Sons Wedding Tea

The Read Everyone has an origin they can point to, the first time the ordinary cracked open and let something better through. Before the title, before the ritual, before any of it became a whole way of being a person, there was just one cup that made you stop and think, oh, this is different. You spend years afterward elaborating on that moment, dressing it up, building a personality on top of it. But it always traces back to the same small unguarded second when something plain turned out, without warning, to be a door. It came in a silk sachet when you were still drinking dust, vanilla rising off it like the inside of a bakery you weren't sure you were allowed to stand in, and it steeped slow and smelled like dessert and asked nothing of you but that you sit down for the length of it. That was the whole seduction. Not strength, not novelty, just a small fragrant moment of calm that turned out to be the first step of a very long walk. You come back to it on your birthday the way you come back to anything that loved you before you knew to be grateful: quietly, a little undone, and glad it is still made. --- The Letter [!dispatch] A Birthday and the One That Started It All Dear Devoted Drinkers of the Leaf, This month, we're making a shift. Not a big one, but a deliberate one. You see, while Harney & Sons has been the backbone of our tea journey (and a frequent flyer in your mailboxes), July calls for something a little different. Because July, dear friends, is my birthday month. And what better time to return to the tea that started it all? Tea of the Month: Mighty Leaf Vanilla Bean Let's rewind. Once upon a time, before I was forcing monthly teas upon innocent friends, before I declared myself your Tea Master, and long before there was a website or a logo or an official(ish) seal… there was Mighty Leaf Vanilla Bean. It was the first tea that made me pause and think, "Oh… this is different." It came in a silken sachet, with real leaves you could actually see—no sad, dusty fragments hiding in a wrinkled paper bag. It smelled like dessert. It steeped like a luxury. This wasn't just tea. This was an experience. A small, fragrant moment of calm. A gentle nudge into the world of fancier brews and proper steeps. So this month, in celebration of my continued aging and our shared descent into tea snobbery, I'm sharing the blend that first made me fall in love with the ritual. Expect a smooth, rich vanilla black tea that's warm and cozy, yet somehow refined. A little nostalgic. A little indulgent. Basically: me in tea form. We'll be back to new discoveries next month. But for now, raise your mugs in honor of birthdays, beginnings, and the perfectly smooth comfort of vanilla. Steeped in sentiment (but not too much), Elder Leaf of the Birthday Blend --- Field Notes - Leaf: 1 silk sachet (or 1 tsp loose) per cup - Water: just off the boil, 95 °C / 205 °F - Steep: 3–4 minutes - Finish: plain lets the vanilla sit; a little milk makes it dessert Acquisition - Direct from Mighty Leaf - The exact sachets: Mighty Leaf Vanilla Bean

The Read Some things walk into the room wearing too much cologne and refuse to apologize for it. You will love them or you will hate them, and there is no province in between, no polite middle where everyone gets along. There is a strange honesty in a thing that divisive, that certain of itself, that fundamentally uninterested in being liked. Most of what we drink is built to please the room. Once in a while something arrives that would rather be itself than be wanted, and stands there daring you to decide which of you you are. It comes in loud, before the water even, smoke and bourbon and char like someone dropped a barrel into a bonfire and bottled the air off the top. The aroma stares back at you. You brew it knowing it might be the worst thing you have ever made or the favorite you never saw coming, and there is no way to learn which until it is on your tongue. That is the dare of the whole thing. Most comfort asks nothing of you. This one asks you to take a side, one smoky cup at a time, and then live with the answer. --- The Letter [!dispatch] The Month of Strong Opinions and Even Stronger Aromas This month's pick? It's… divisive. It's dramatic. It's walking into a room wearing too much cologne. Tea of the Month: Harney & Sons Black Cask Bourbon Even before brewing, this tea comes in loud. The aroma hits you like it's got something to prove—smoky, boozy, bold, and just a little bit unhinged. It's like someone dropped a bourbon barrel into a bonfire, then bottled the air and said, "Here. Make tea with this." Now, to be fair, this isn't a bad tea. It's a very specific tea. It knows what it is and makes no apologies. And while I personally can't get behind it flavor-wise, I'm fascinated by it. Because here's the thing: There's a real possibility that one of you will absolutely love it. Maybe you're a smoky tea person. Maybe you love bold flavors that punch you in the mouth. Maybe this is the tea that pairs perfectly with the exact bourbon in your cabinet. Maybe this becomes your unexpected favorite and you feel seen. So this month, I'm not offering a cozy memory or a nostalgic backstory—I'm offering a challenge. Brew it. Try it. And tell me where you land: - "This is weird and I kind of love it." - "This is weird and I kind of hate it." - "What even is this and why is it staring into my soul?" There's no right answer—only honest steeping. Here's to stepping outside our comfort zone, one smoky cup at a time. Cautiously caffeinated, Curator of Conflicted Leaves --- Field Notes - Leaf: 1 tsp loose (or 1 bag) per cup - Water: full boil, 100 °C / 212 °F - Steep: 4–5 minutes; it is bold, it will not get shy - Finish: plain to meet it head-on, or milk to talk it down a little Acquisition - Direct from Harney & Sons - The exact tin: Harney & Sons Black Cask Bourbon

The Read There is a specific want that only surfaces when you have nothing left. Not the big hungers, the loud ones you announce to a room. The small exact one that claws its way up through exhaustion and grief and the dirt you have your face in, and what it asks for is absurdly precise: not comfort in general but this one particular thing, named, longed for, shouted through tears while the world watches and fails to understand. The specificity is the whole point. When everything else has burned off, what remains is the true small craving, and it is never vague. It steeps up jade-green and shade-grown, thick with a savory umami that ordinary tea never bothers to reach for, faintly sweet underneath, the cup somebody wept for mid-collapse because some animal part of him already knew it was worth weeping for. You drink it in the quiet, or you drink it rage-chewing rice in solidarity with every exhausted creature who ever wanted one good and specific thing in the middle of being unmade. Either way it delivers exactly what was promised. The longing was not unhinged. The longing knew precisely what it was after, and it was this. --- The Letter [!dispatch] And I Want High-Quality Gyokuro Tea Dear Comrades of the Cup, September's tea is inspired by the purest expression of emotional meltdown ever animated: Tanjiro Kamado, exhausted and starving, slamming face-first into the dirt while crying, "And I want high-quality gyokuro tea!" For those who need a refresher: this happens during the Swordsmith Village Arc, when young Kotetsu refuses to feed Tanjiro until he manages to land a hit on a six-armed training doll. The tea request? Absolutely unhinged. But also? Deeply relatable. Yes, this is timed with the release of Infinity Castle. Yes, we are absolutely those people now. And yes—Alicia still hasn't watched Demon Slayer. (Yes, Alicia. We love her, but she still hasn't watched Demon Slayer, and at this point, it's starting to feel personal.) Tea of the Month: Tealyra Gyokuro Kokyu Premium This month, we're sipping the very tea Tanjiro longed for through tears and trauma: Gyokuro. A shade-grown, umami-rich Japanese green tea that's smooth, savory, and slightly sweet in the way that only high-quality green tea can be. We're drinking Tealyra's Kokyu Premium, wrapped in pyramid-style sachets that feel worthy of their own anime transformation sequence. Best enjoyed in a moment of quiet… or while rage-chomping rice balls and pickled plums in full solidarity with Tanjiro. I discovered this tea solely because I needed to know what could possibly drive someone to shout it mid-breakdown. And now, you get to join me in that enlightenment. So steep with honor. Sip with pride. And if you hear a distant voice yelling, "Tasty," that's just the spirit of Tanjiro reminding you to appreciate your tea—and your snacks. In anime-fueled serenity, Hashira of the High-Quality Leaves --- Field Notes - Leaf: 1 sachet (or 1 tsp loose) per small cup; gyokuro likes a generous leaf-to-water ratio - Water: cool, 60 °C / 140 °F. This is the one tea you do not pour boiling water on - Steep: 1–2 minutes; short and low brings the umami, heat and time bring bitterness - Finish: plain, always. Never milk. Re-steep the leaves; the second cup is often the best Acquisition - Direct from Tealyra - The exact sachets: Tealyra Gyokuro Kokyu Premium

The Read There is no shame in wanting the obvious comfort. The crowd-pleaser. The thing every marketing department on earth already decided you would love, that you love anyway, fully aware you have been handed it, because some warmth is real even when it is manufactured. Autumn does this to everyone. It comes for you with cinnamon and early dark and the particular ache of a season that smells like memory, and you do not fight it. You let yourself be the easy mark. You light the candle you know is a cliché and you feel better regardless. It steeps up spiced and amber, clove and cinnamon and that soft pumpkin warmth you were taught to crave and now crave sincerely, the cup that smells like October moved into your kitchen and hung a scarf by the door. You make it a latte because the day was long and you have earned a small softness. You wrap yourself in a blanket like the haunted little teacake you are, and you pour hot water over dried leaves in the oldest small ritual there is, the one that was always half spell, and for the length of a cup you are a potion master and the only spell is this: stay warm, stay here, the dark outside can keep. --- The Letter [!dispatch] Spooky Steeps and the Curse of the Redundant Chai Dear Creatures of the Night (and also, tea lovers), The shadows are long, the air is crisp, and something stirs in your mailbox… It's time for the Spooky Season Steep, and this month, we're embracing the cozy chaos of fall with a blend that smells like October wrapped itself in a scarf and moved into your kitchen. Tea of the Month: Twinings Chai Pumpkin Spice It's warm. It's spicy. It's full of cinnamon and clove and "accidentally bought four pumpkins again" energy. This tea is a seasonal classic that somehow manages to feel like it should be served next to a bonfire while wearing a chunky knit sweater. This blend brings that classic masala chai warmth, layered with the soft pumpkin spice vibes we've all been indoctrinated into loving by seasonal marketing and our own nostalgia. It's sweet, fragrant, and honestly kind of perfect for sipping while watching spooky movies or judging Halloween costumes too harshly. Will this be the most daring tea we try? Absolutely not. Is it a crowd-pleaser with haunted vibes and cozy comfort in a cup? Yes. So steep it strong, toss in some milk to make it a latte, and wrap yourself in a blanket like the haunted little teacake you are. This Halloween, the real magic isn't in costumes or candy. It's in the ritual of pouring hot water over dried leaves and pretending you're a potion master. Witchfully yours, Conjurer of Cozy Cauldrons --- Field Notes - Leaf: 1 bag (or 1 tsp loose) per cup - Water: full boil, 100 °C / 212 °F; chai wants the heat to open the spice - Steep: 4–5 minutes, longer if you are taking it as a latte - Finish: milk and a little sweetener; froth it if you are feeling like a potion master Acquisition - Direct from Twinings , or any grocery tea aisle in autumn - The exact box: Twinings Chai Pumpkin Spice

03:01 HST The heat sticks to my body like a bad passenger — the kind of waking sleep paralysis that sinks onto your chest and doesn't let you move. My body is still calibrated to Seattle cold and time, and even though it's pitch dark outside, for me it's no different than any other day. I've become used to waking before the sun, as if trying to prove a point — arriving five minutes early even when sunrise isn't due for another three hours. This morning feels less peaceful. Sporadic. Anxiety-filled. As if the locals will soon find out I've taken residence in one of their homes and come to evict me with smiles and half-tossed jokes. The house sleeps, and with a light foot I make my way through the rooms looking for a single 1/8 screw to fix a coffee machine I jerry-rigged not to look like a bomb for customs, reaching for a semblance of normality I know won't ever come. They say it's bad luck to carry anything home from the island, but I'm hoping that temporarily borrowing it won't add to my string of bad luck. The sun here stretches as unhurried as the rest of the island. Long gone is the rush of responsibilities, the compulsion to get everything done before 0800 just to feel productive before jumping on the late capitalist hamster wheel that's teetering on breaking at the axle. Things here have a way of sinking you into the moment. The lashing of ocean I can hear from two blocks down. The way the sun rises slowly, unhurried, as if saying: "See, I'm here, right where I need to be. I just took my time getting here." Even the wind feels like a languid stretch compared to the violent punches of the Chicago breeze. I feel at odds here. A part of me wanting to slip back into bed, to arrive slowly and without pretense, to just be in the moment. And the other primal part of my brain ringing the alarms that I need to be productive, that things need to get done because tomorrow will never come. So I've forced myself to compromise. A single entry of a morning I can't begin to understand in a place I can't call home, and a stolen cup of coffee with a hint of rust at the bottom from the half-acquired screw. Wondering, curious even, if I'll ever find myself not rushing toward the exit, but just waiting, staying in the moment before the sun breaks over the ocean.

13:32 HST When you land, the first thing that hits you is vegetal rot and frangipani — the sickly sweet way the whole island sweats on you as soon as you exit the plane. There's no metallic birth canal from airplane to terminal. Just the whining, aching ramp that's ten years too old and twenty years overused, rust blooming under the handrails like a fungus. But you can't help but smile as the Hawaiian sky stretches overhead. The exodus of passengers as they make their way to baggage claim. Lost and smiling idiots as they pick up their bags from the screeching belt, rusted and pelted by salt air, as it begrudgingly snakes their luggage past. The ramp agent watches from behind kind eyes as he moves on to the next arrival while I already lay forgotten. He has seen this exodus five times daily. He is not impressed. The humid air clings to the half-remembered page shoved in the back of a pocket — the most recommended places, given to those that ask like a prayer card during mass.

14:14 HST The road winds upward and the hours stretch thin like the air around us, as if time itself has been roasted along with the beans, expanding, darkening, taking up more space than it should. We feel the ascent not in our lungs but in the thickness behind our eyes, the way the heat seems to press against the windshield not from above but from below, rising through the floorboards like the island is exhaling through the metal of the rental. The barracks slide past, weather-beaten things that have surrendered to the sun’s slow interrogation, paint peeling from lumber, a testament to storms that have argued with them and won, leaving them hollowed out, creaking and waiting under the sun. At the summit, the farm terraces down in a green ziggurat, each row precise, mathematical, a geometry of belief cut into the slope. The guides emerge with smiles that haven’t yet learned the fatigue of repetition, new enough to the liturgy that “terroir” still tastes like honey in their mouths. All three move in synchronization, ten days and three months and five years all wearing the same expression, a uniform of optimism that feels almost inherited. We roast beans in the drum-heat under a wooden lanai, a concentrated heat like an iron skillet that is different from the wet pregnant air outside; dry, metallic, a heat that scrapes rather than suffocates; and for a moment the brightness of the cup they hand us makes us forget the barracks below, the trailer park gathering like driftwood at the base of the hill, the live-work arithmetic of survival. Afterwards they award us our own labor, sealed and labeled, as they escort us to a small coffee house just a few steps away. A child waits behind us. Five years old, maybe six, standing at the edge of the doorway with two dollars crushed in his fist like a secret. He is staring at the lemonade with the focused hunger of someone who has not yet learned that money and desire rarely align. Two dollars is not enough, but he doesn’t know the grammar of transaction yet. The barista takes our order and then concentrates on her new client, fills the cup anyway, hands it down. The boy drinks without audience. There is no camera, no “do you like it,” no proxy to perform satisfaction for, just the sugar and the water and the sun baking his forehead. We take our own cup from the woman behind the counter. Her smile is thin, weighed down by the gravity of accumulated days spent watching tourists photograph their own enjoyment. She doesn’t recite. She doesn’t perform the catechism. She just hands us the paper cup, and we feel the heat of it pinching our fingertips, a small pain that grounds us. We realize we are holding our faces in that automatic rictus, the smile we wear so strangers know we are not a threat, so the island knows we are grateful, so we ourselves believe we are having the experience we paid for. The coffee is good. It is acid-bright, alive like a siren song in a paper cup, and as we drink we feel the slow pull of guilt setting its teeth into us, not for being here, but for being here badly, for feeling the hamster wheel still turning in our chests even as the sun attempts to bake it still, to slow the blood in our ears to the rhythm of the farm, the ziggurat, the indifferent birds that chirp their backing track without knowing if we are local or tourist or just another mammal in the heat. The boy finishes his lemonade. He does not look at us. He is not wondering if he is enjoying it efficiently enough, if he is extracting the proper value per dollar, if he should be documenting this for someone else. He is simply sweating, glowing, being stolen from by the sun and the sugar in equal measure, while we stand there with the full cup in hand, aware of the bin, the road winding down, the ocean somewhere below waiting to wash away the thought we can’t quite name. The one about how soon we'll return to a place called home.

08:37 HST I changed my pants. Finally. The old ones were developing sentience in the humidity, plotting rebellion against my thighs. Three hours ago—my body still insists it was six—the sky electrocuted the ocean. Dry lightning. Thirty thousand volts striking water that didn't flinch. I stood on the lanai in fresh darkness, showered and deluded, watching the horizon commit violence without thunder. The house slept its air-conditioned sleep. I was the only witness, which means it didn't happen. Now the grinder. I turn the handle and the beans crack like small bones. Too loud. The island has rules about noise that the wildlife enforce with silence. Birds and monkeys somewhere beyond the manicured lawn, listening to see if I'm worth the trouble. I grind slower, trying to hide the sound beneath the humidity. It doesn't work. They know I'm here and join in the cacophony The house pretends. Fresh beige paint over God-knows-what. White baseboards that have never known a scuff. But the ceiling fans tell the truth—rusted with blooming corrosion, spinning like they know the power could cut any second. One light fixture has already given up. It hangs there, dead, while the others perform. The coffee blooms. Local, glistening with the tell tale signs of freshly roasted, or maybe it to is a victim of the humidity of this place . Somebody's labor, left behind ,so I can feel authentic before 9 AM. My hands smell like the beans and the specific shame of being awake when the island isn't watching. The library of nature continues it incessant song. It knows I'm here.

10:21 HST The rain hit like a dropped pallet of bricks. No mist, no polite Seattle warning: just the sky opening its payload bay and dumping. Seattle rain is a roommate who doesn't do dishes: constant, low-grade resentment that soaks into the drywall. You stop noticing it after three days. You develop a pathology. Hawaiian rain is a SWAT team. It arrives, secures the perimeter, and leaves. Right now it is ball-bearinging off the lanai roof, overwhelming the gutters—hardware designed for polite precipitation—so they vomit water onto the concrete with structural panic. My phone pings with the already late news that there's a flash flood incoming. Rain hits the pool like buckshot. Craters open and vanish—water assaulting water, a drive-by shooting that leaves no witnesses, no evidence to catalog. The surface is amnesiac, swallowing each impact before the next one lands. I stand under the canvas, dry and fraudulent, watching the gutters overflow like-drunkards. The gardener has fled to his truck, mission accomplished, tires leaving mud that will be erased by noon. And I admire the half hearted suicide of every droplet. The birds have flown away. The geckos have ceased their patrols. It's just me and the rain, and the rain will always win.

13:12 HST The rental cuts a cone through the rain—no trees, no moss, no canopy to catch the light. Just void. On either side, the S3 - Lava Fields extend like a scrapyard for the obsolete: black, clinker-sharp, absorbing the high beams like a debt. Mile after mile of cooled violence, sharp enough to shred a tire, old enough to predate the concept of trespassing. There are no landmarks. The horizon is a mathematics of slope and shadow. Back home, the roads apologize. They hide inside Douglas fir and devil's club, embarrassed by their own utility. Guardrails rust quietly into the undergrowth, reclaimed by moss within a season. Nature tolerates the interstate by digesting it. Here, the highway is a suture on a wound that refuses to heal. It sits on top, unhidden, the white center lines glaring like surgical tape on a burn victim. Engineers drew a straight line through the void with the arrogance of a cartographer mapping hell, and now I traverse it at sixty miles an hour, air conditioning on, isolated from the heat that still radiates from the cracks below the asphalt. We keep driving. The white lines disappear behind me, swallowed by the dark like a suture getting pulled through a wound that won't close.

14:20 HST S3 - Pine Tree Cafe squats between a local mart selling beach chairs that'll break by Tuesday and a church offering salvation with one hand and collection plates with the other. I came here because the line was short and my self respect was shorter. The menu is a war crime of laminated plastic. I am wearing joggers that have never seen a jog and a moisture-wicking shirt of a lie that barely holds back my impending dehydration. I tell myself I'm here for the local experience, not because I haven't eaten since 9 AM. The waitress has heard this lie twenty times before lunch. She looks at me like I'm a problem she already solved. She is not wrong. The locals will tell you you're welcome while drinking ice cold chili water strong enough to exorcise demons—served in plastic cups like communion shots, as if spice could absolve the sins I've committed by noon. It won't be enough. It will have to be. The sides arrive as a trinity: potato, tuna, and macaroni—three salads, zero vegetables, a mathematical impossibility that somehow equals lunch.

19:45 HST The tourist center squats at the end of a road that dies in gravel, red roof shedding shingles like a disease. The owner did the math: why pay to repair what the next hurricane will scatter across the parking lot? The tar paper underneath has bubbled in the heat, blistered, waiting. Inside, the macadamia nuts sit in plastic cans, sweating their oil, waiting for a power grid that flickers. The road ends in gravel that forces you to walk the last fifty yards like a trespasser approaching a crime scene. The silence isn't contemplative; it's the sound of a place waiting for the next disaster. No wind. Just the compressor-knock of waves against lava rocks—machinery failing at the shoreline. Plywood covers windows that broke in 2008. The parking lot asphalt is crumbling into the lava rock substrate—slow erosion, mechanical failure at the molecular level. Outside, the waves. They do not sound like traffic. Traffic is aluminum impatience, a mechanical whine. This is white noise from a compressor that has been running since before the concept of rent. The water hits the lava rocks with the persistence of a debt collector—no anger, just the job, older than the concrete jetty that tried to stop it and failed. The sun is setting with the chemical indifference of a sodium flare. Orange, yellow, blue—colors that require no interpretation, only acknowledgment that the day shift is ending. Four rowboats cross the horizon. They move perpendicular to the sunset, hulls low in the water, either returning or escaping. I should buy a postcard. I don't. The shop isn't closed; it's simply given up on the distinction.

06:00 HST Hours seem longer on the island, as if time itself stretches languidly in the sun. I'm on the lanai with nothing to do and nowhere to be, warm heat baking skin, birds chirping like a backing track of nature, the sound of all these little worlds all around you happening in a microcosm that have never given a thought of you as a local or a tourist, just another body in the sun. And as the hours stretch with nothing to do, a tense body finally allows itself to rest, to drift into a sleep where the dreams are illuminated by the 1 PM sun. Night here is no different. You can feel it on the roads, in the people all around you, the way that each step is just a bit slower. It's as if the island, with its own sun-fueled gravity, forces you to witness it — every crash of a wave, every breeze that sways the tree, every crack on the sidewalk, every bead of sweat that has a 50/50 chance of dripping down or being baked on your forehead. You move through the tide of sun-baked roads and for once you don't hear the high-speed pumping of blood in your ears, thoughts come slower and you forget why you ever wanted to be fast and efficient in the first place. The slow pull of guilt sets its teeth into you, if only to allow a bit more sun to sink into Seattle-logged bones. And even with the sun hitting your face, as if it could dry away years of rain, your mind wanders to all the things you have left to do, all the things that need your attention. And then you hear the ocean, its waves rushing in as if to wash away the thoughts, and something has been stolen from you that you can't name. The sun feels like a siren's song as it lulls you back into the sweet languish of nothing. There's a shack where they sell crushed ice and syrup, the kind of sweet decadence you can only find in paradise. A family of five making their order as a mom video calls her husband and shows him how much his child is enjoying the treat, and for a moment I felt like an outsider looking in. There was an undercurrent of anxiety, this need for even something as small as this to be efficient — "Do you like it, tell him how much you like it, what's your favorite flavor?" — a barrage of questions which normally I wouldn't pay a second mind to. As if by answering them the father could vicariously enjoy the moment, a mother who in guilt wants to say "Thank you for this, see how he enjoys it, sorry that you're not here," but communication has fallen to the wayside and now we only speak to each other through proxies and screens. But in that moment the child and I were one and the same. There was no worry about how much I was enjoying it, or how efficiently I was eating it, it didn't even matter how sticky our hands got or if we dropped some on the ground for the local wildlife to share. There was just this, this moment as the questions washed over us both, and I began to wonder when did we lose that? The ability to just be in the moment and enjoy that bite, not worrying about putting on a show for another or performing how good or bad a thing was. Just one bite, nothing more than fruit, water, and sugar and the sun hitting your face. I smile, because you always smile at strangers so they don't think of you as a threat, and took one last bite. Not for them, not because it was expected, but just to be in that moment; to recapture the wonder of trying something new, something as ordinary as sugar and water on a surfboard of a spoon.

16:05 HST The only empty driveway at the end of a cul de sac that both remind me that I'm a stranger while trying its hardest to convince me that I'm welcome. The door swing quietly as the cool stink of recycled air left behind by the last lucky traveler quickly pushes past me in a rush to leave this house performing a home, or a hotel denying its own name. The house is clean. Like a crimes scene after the fact hoping for more fingerprints to layer over its dark secrets. 3 rooms, each one smaller than the next all promising to keep you safe while you call this home. The fan overhead wobbles and creaks under its own wait with the same anxious whine of the blinker if a totaled car. The backyard sells paradise with palm trees and a pool left half-cleaned by a keeper too weary to scrub the ring around the tile—proof that even paradise requires labor, and labor has a 3 PM energy crash.

10:00 HST It’s a comfort to know that the heat of the island — the organized humidity, the way the earth itself sweats — is shared by the very people that inhabit it. We enter Panulu’u Bakery and the face of the woman who serves us is flushed with a heat that betrays it’s more than the ovens, her skin already accustomed to the heat of this place and its proofing boxes. The sweet smell of yeast and sugar tempts you from the parking lot. The cashier already looks dogged and tired as he thanks us for the purchase, just another face in a sea of customers as they tackle two different lines. It’s only been about an hour in the car on a three-hour journey and him and I share the same face. Flushed cheeks, brows heavy with sweat, dark Atlases trying to carry the weight of the heat on our shoulders. But even this pilgrimage to the other side of the island is worth it as your teeth sink into the malasada. A cloud of sugar, fruit, and bread. Sweet enough to send you into a diabetic coma, but not sweet enough to stop you from wanting another. It’s curious to see the varied brushstrokes of people that line the sidewalk as they wait. Even the ants make a foray under one of the canopies, pillaging the floor for crumbs left by departing faithfuls, carrying a piece of yeast and sugar five times their size back to their cave. A group of bakers shelter at the back under the shade of a massive Banyan tree, its branches rising and intertwining as if it too wants to build a wall against the heat. Even in the heat they sit close together, their laughter ringing out — small and bright against the loud machine that kneads the dough and the sweltering ovens. We’ve only been driving an hour. We have two more to go.

The air is thin. Your lungs struggling to hold as much oxygen as they can while your brain panics and slowly begins to slip agreeableness. The cold barely scratches the surface, nothing more than the brisk of a Seattle morning on the top of the world's tallest mountain, and it only took a bus ride to get there. At this height, it's easy to understand why every depiction of heaven has always been painted as above the clouds. This far up, the clouds sit below you in a rolling carpet that dwarfs the world beneath. Nothing but you and the sky above as the sun begins to crack. It bleeds as if the galaxy itself were hemorrhaging against the purple and blue of a sky that's taken a day to breathe. The world darkens, but it's not the slow dwindling of light — it's fast and by several degrees, like the earth is gasping out the light. A slew of cars holding the line at the edge of the mountain. An audience to the coming dark. Phone and camera lenses, witnesses to a dying sun as it falls beneath the earth. And then the cold begins to settle deeper into your bones. Above, a single tree huddles against the observatory as if shielding itself in defiance. The world above the clouds feels like a different world, one not meant for human consumption, and it's difficult not to feel like a thief in the night stealing a glance at the firmament, or a god standing at its edge.

12:00 HST A quick stop at Panalu'u beach. Black sands and obsidian shores. A sticker, and a bottle of the ink-dark sand as a keepsake — not to remember that beaches differ, but maybe just to remember the four sea turtles I noticed as I walked in, motionless on the shore like stones that learned to breathe. The sands are blistering, making their way through the grips of my shoes. My legs ache, my back hurts, and all I can think of is the heat, and the way that even the water here would betray me — cold, sharp, indifferent. The shores are littered with tourists who, just like me, are curious about these shores. In the distance you can hear the laughter as diver after diver throws their body off a cliff into the cold waters below. Two hours later I arrive at the twin furnaces of . Thirty dollars is the price of entry to view the island's forge. The visitors center is closed for renovations, and the welcome center is little more than a help desk attached to a gift shop fronting military barracks that remind me of basic. As if on cue the old mannerisms sink back in. No covers inside. Lockstep to the person at your side. The drizzle at four thousand feet is a welcome reprieve from the seventy-five degree heat. Seventy-five on the mainland is nothing, but at this elevation where oxygen is thinner than a thread and you feel closer to the sun, seventy-five feels like ninety in the humidity with no breeze. The volcano is quiet, resting — a nap before its violent awakening — and if you look long enough you can almost see the rising steam of its snore at the far end of the crater. Below, I descend into Nāhuku. A single cave structure that extends for a quarter mile beneath the earth. The lights are snuffed — either because the volcano forgot to pay its light bill or just because the parks are short on funding. Into the dark we travel, phone torches in hand, as the earth around us moves. A thin wall of solidified magma older than the roads it took us to get here, and for a moment you can hear movement behind the walls through the crack of the cave. The darkness is absolute, the stone warm against your palm. There's something almost holy about walking through a lava tube in the dark. The same reverence as when you step into a cathedral. It feels old, ancient, as if reminding you that the blood that courses through this island is not just heat, but fire. You keep walking.

I woke between two red mountains. No ship. No name in my mouth. No companion. A body — mine. The feeling of something that had been at my side and was not. The badlands were still. Waiting. The sky the colour of a bruise. The wind off the cliff did not say my name. I do not remember what was lost. Only that it was. And that it had the shape of someone. Before shelter. Before water. Before direction. Stone. Stacked. A torch on top. Not a grave. Nothing to bury. Not a shrine. No one to pray to. A signature. I was here. This is where I began. Across the river — a town. Small. Smoke the wrong colour for the hour. Faces in the windows. They neither waved nor turned. I did not cross. The call was already pulling east. I do not know the town's name. I do not know if it has one. I will return when I have something to bring back. The dream did not end when I woke. That is the first thing the badlands taught me. The dream does not end. It only changes who is in it with you. I left the mark. I left the river. I walked east. The red mountains let me go. They had never been asked to hold me.

The city had no other trade. Every door opened on a forge. I came in at dusk. Three days of walking. The bellows were louder than the wind before I crossed the bridge. Hammers on iron. Hammers on iron. The streets breathed to it. A bed in a room at the edge of town. Small. A chest. A double door — to keep me from the outside, or the outside from me. I did not learn which. The walls were warm to the touch. I slept like the dead. The noise was a kind of mother. Storms on the second night. Lightning over the castle on the far bank. No one in the city looked across. The castle was a fact, like weather. I did not cross. I did not ask. Two days. Bread. Flint. A pot for water. The shape of a routine. I let myself believe in walls. On the third morning the call came back. Not a voice. A weight behind the breastbone — a compass leaning. Somewhere else. Somewhere more right. A direction without a name. I left before I could talk myself into staying. Bought bread I did not eat. Told the innkeeper a lie about my route. Walked out the eastern gate while the smiths still slept. The hammers started again before I crossed the second hill. The wound of leaving is its own season. I am still in it.

The valley was full of blue light before I saw what was bleeding. Trees the colour of bone. The ground ran blue with small flowers I had no name for. They did flicker. Flowers should not flicker. Then the body. Or what is left of one. Hung on a beam too tall for the sky to have built. I did not look long. A tower on a hill to the east. Lit. Tended. Lights that did not waver in the wind. Kept by someone — or keeping itself. I did not approach either. I climbed. The house is inside the mountain. A tunnel at the foot. An elevator. Rooms that had been kept , not used. A bed made by someone else. A cup on a table, the dust undisturbed around it. Armor on a stand sized for a body not quite mine. The windows are wrong for light. Right for watching. From the bed, hidden behind a wall, as if it wanted to see but not be seen by the corpse that hung from the pylon — the lit tower to the east. Through the same frame, the thing on the beam to the south. Whoever cut these windows could not choose which was the threat. I cannot either. At the mountain's foot, a flight of stone steps. Older than the slope they sit on. They climb nothing. They begin in the earth and end in the air. Something stood here before the mountain. Or the mountain came up around it. The stones do not say. The house had been holding its breath. It let it out when I crossed the threshold. It did not love the one before me. It does not love me. It needed me. That is a different thing. Tonight I will sleep in the bed someone else made. I will try not to wake to the sound of someone trying to come home.

I heard them before I saw them. Crossbow strings. Boots on stone. Voices not pitched for prayer. I had been watching the lit tower from the bedroom window — the way a man watches a candle he did not light, to see if it will go out. It was going out. Down through the tunnel. Across the field. The blue flowers gave under my feet without complaint. She was on the steps when I came up the hill. The woman in the tower. The keeper of the lights. Something in her hand — a small book, or a leaf torn from one — held the way you hold the last thing. Five of them. Iron crossbows. An axe. A fourth came late from the trees holding a Grey Banner like a shield over its head. I did not see her fall. I saw the lights inside the tower flicker once. That was all. What was done after that I will not write. The body remembers without my help. When it was done I went inside. The lights were still burning. The tower had not noticed. On her desk: a book. Large. Open. Pages in a language I have never seen. Letters that leaned the wrong way. Diagrams. A drawing of the thing on the beam in a hand that had spent years getting it right. I closed the book. I did not take it. I do not read what is not mine. Not yet. The tower was clean. Tended. A bed I could have slept in. A hearth. Bread under a cloth. The kind of place that wanted to be lived in by someone who knew what it was for. It was not me. I left the doors as I found them. I left her where she had fallen. I gathered her things and hid them in a chest. Walking back down the hill the lights were still burning behind me. I did not turn to look. Someone is keeping them lit. It is not her anymore.

I went south because I had not yet. Past the lit tower. Down through the white forest. Bone trees and flickering flowers and a silence that made my boots loud. Nothing came out to look at me. Nothing has come out yet. The forest is empty the way certain rooms are empty — the kind where someone has just left. I came to the lake before I came to the pylon. The water was dark. Still. Fed from beneath. No stream ran in. No stream ran out. Looking at it was not like looking at water. It was like looking at a window into something that had decided to look back. I did not drink. I walked around it on the western shore. Then the body. Then the beam. Bigger close than from the bedroom window. Bigger than any beam should be. Bigger than the sky should have allowed. Half of him is on it still — the ribcage, the skull, an arm. The rest has come down into the ground over the years it took to fall. The trees grew out of what fell. The flowers grew where the blood went. The pylon is hollow. I did not expect that. A way in at the base — not a door anyone cut. The earth has opened where it meets the stone. Inside: air. Stone. A staircase. The staircase spirals down. I walked the first turn. Then another. Then another. I stopped counting. Each layer gave up something different the further I went. Pale stone. Coal in threads through the wall. Then iron in veins like cold blood. Deeper — gold, soft and yellow as a lie. Deeper — redstone, the colour of a thing that wants to be a heart and is not. Then lapis, blue the way the flowers above are blue. Deeper still — diamond. Set into the rock in places I did not expect. The way teeth are set into a jaw. I made an altar at each. Stone. A torch. A small offering of what I had taken. I do not know who I made them for. The pylon felt like a thing that should be answered. I had nothing else to answer it with. The spiral reaches bedrock. I touched it. The world ended there in the only way a world ends — by refusing to let me go further. There was more at the bottom than rock. Shelves. Rows of them, broken and swollen, sunk halfway into the floor. A library, or what a library becomes when it is left at the bottom of the world. The books had gone to something that was no longer reading. I did not touch them. And on the lowest step — something pale, and something dark spread around it that I told myself was shadow. I did not go to it. I had come down to dig, not to disturb whatever had been laid there. I told myself I would come back for it. I will come back is the most dangerous sentence I know. I have started saying it about everything. The whole climb back I could not shake the thought. The pylon is a cathedral. I do not know who built it that way. I do not know if anyone built it. The staircase is not a thing the builders of the beam would have needed. The beam carries a god. The staircase carries the worshipper. Someone has been worshipping. They are not here now. I went up. The torches I had left were still burning. The altars were still standing. The lake at the surface was still still. The body had not moved. Of course it had not moved. But I checked.

I opened the chest tonight. I had not meant to. I had meant to walk past it, the way I have walked past it every night since the tower. Her things are not many. A comb. A knife worn thin. A bundle of dried blue flowers that have not wilted and will not. And the leaf — the one she held on the steps, torn from something larger along a ragged edge. I took it from her hand that day. I could not leave the last thing she chose to hold lying in the dirt for the crows. I held it to the lamp. I cannot read it. The letters lean the wrong way, the way they leaned in the book on her desk. They are not my letters. They are barely letters at all — more a thing remembered than a thing written. But there is a shape in the middle of the page that I know. Water. A ring of water with nothing flowing in and nothing flowing out. And beneath it, a mark pressed so hard the pen went through — three times, in the same place, as though she could not make her hand stop. I have stood at that water. I know the one she means. She died holding this. A woman I never spoke to gave the last of herself to a page I cannot read. I do not know what it says. I know only that she thought it worth more than the breath it cost her. I have put it in my coat. Not the chest. My coat. I keep things I cannot read now. I had one already — a loss with no name, carried since the badlands, that I cannot make out no matter how I turn it to the light. This is the second. They sit together well enough. When I learn to read her letters, I will know what she died for. I am not certain I want to be the kind of man who knows.

I went back down for the thing I left on the step. I have learned that the sentences I am most afraid of are the ones I have to keep. The shelves at the bottom are a library, drowned. Monks' work, I think — who else carries books to the floor of the world? They came down here to read their way out of something, and it did not work, and the books have gone soft as silt. All but one. The one sat apart, on the lowest shelf, dry in a wet world, set down with care for a hand that never closed on it. I have it now. I cannot read it. The letters lean the wrong way — her letters. The tower's letters. The letters of the page in my coat. Whatever this book is, it was meant for someone who could read her language. And on the step below it: the dark I told myself was shadow. It was not shadow. It is old now, and dry, and there is a great deal of it — more than a person could lose and still walk away. But there is no one here. No body. Only the blood, and scattered through it like leaves after a storm, pages. A man's hand, this time. Plain. Mine. Letters I can read. And above the worst of it, the book, set down careful and untouched, as though whatever happened here had no interest in the book at all. I read the pages on the step, out of order, in the blood they were lying in. A man kept a watch here. He was the thirtieth to keep it. He gave his name up at the door of the house — my house, the bed I sleep in, the armour on the stand I have never been able to make fit. He watched the body on the beam, and the water, and the tower on the hill. And the woman in it. He loved her. He did not say it for thirty pages and then he could not stop. He had spoken to her eleven times. He counted. And the one night he let himself hope, he came down here to bring her a book from the bottom of the world — this book, the dry one, the one in my hands — because she was the only soul alive who could read it, and he wanted to put something in her hands that no one else could give. He was going to climb up and tell her. The last page says so. The last page stops in the middle of saying so. there was a man who— And then, lower, in a different press of the pen, as though something in the room had changed: In three years it has never once That is where it ends. That is where he ended. There were other pages in the blood. About the body, and the dark beneath the water, and what he had decided the two of them were to each other. I read them twice. I could not make them mean anything but a lonely man's madness — the kind that grows in a person kept too long alone with something too large. Perhaps that is all they are. Perhaps I am only not ready to read them as anything else. But the rest I know, because I was there for the part he never reached. The woman he loved is the woman I watched die on the tower steps with a torn page in her hand. He went down to bring her a book; she went down holding a secret; and neither of them ever knew the other had gone. Two people an inch apart for years, and the inch was never closed, and now it never will be. I live in his house. I sleep in his bed. I have his book I cannot read, and her page I cannot read, and a grief of my own I cannot read, and tonight I carried all three of them up the stair together, because I could not think what else to do with them. I keep telling myself I came to this valley to begin again. I think I came here to be the one who finds the ends of other people's sentences.

CAELEUM, THE HOLLOW HEART Of the Name The first of our line called him Caeleum , after a word in a tongue no longer spoken aloud by anything with a mouth. It is said the word meant the fallen sky, and broken moon. I once made no claim for the etymology. I have since stopped doubting it. Some words are not learned. They are remembered. Other names have been offered him — by travellers, by the frightened, by Keepers before me who could not bear the silence of the first one. The Hung. The Long Vigil. The Martyr. The God that Watches . Each confesses something the speaker did not mean to confess. Caeleum confesses nothing. That is how I know the name is his. Of the Body One hundred and fifty paces from the crown of the pylon to the lowest stone that still clears the earth. Twenty-five across at the shoulder. I have taken these measures more times than the work has ever required. I no longer measure him to learn his size. I measure him the way the devout tell their beads — to have somewhere to put my hands while the rest of me kneels. Both arms remain. The skull. The spine to the waist. The ribcage, open like a hand that has decided not to close. Below the waist he has gone down into the ground, a pelvis and legs that have long since succumbed to gravity and goes down still, slowly, across a count of years I have given up keeping — the way a man lowers himself into dark water he does not intend to leave. What he surrenders to the earth, the earth answers with trees. I have watched a single rib descend the width of my palm across three winters. When the last of him passes beneath the soil I do not know what the valley will become. I have stopped pretending I do not think on it daily. The bone is white as teeth are white, never as paper is. It does not splinter. Strike it and it sings the blow back at you. The grain of wood runs through it where there is no wood, and I no longer find this strange. It is the first sign of the patience. He is becoming the forest, so that the forest may carry forward what he was. Of the Reach — His Body, Continued The white trees are not trees. They are Caeleum, persevering in a humbler medium. They flower without seed. They shed nothing in any season. No bird will nest in them; no creature dens beneath. A trunk that falls will sink into the soil and rise again as a sapling — always further from the body, never toward it. The Pale Reach is widening. Slowly. Patiently. Reaching, as he reaches, for something neither he nor I can name. Bring an axe against the pale oak and it gives a sound no living wood would make. The cut heartwood is the colour of a page that has never been written upon. I confess I have stood with my hand against a fresh stump and felt that I was touching the inside of a hymn. Of the Bloom — His Blood, Made Willing The Veilbloom is the blood that left him and chose not to die with the rest. It is not flower in any sense the herbals would allow. It is liquid that has remembered how to be still. Each petal is blue, with a centre of green that is not colour but light — and the light does not waver, except in places, and in those places I will not kneel, though everything in me wishes to. God forgive me. Everything in me wishes to. A bloom, once picked, holds its shape for as long as the one who picked it wishes it to. It cannot be eaten — and yet I have held one to my lips in the dark of the tower and felt it ask to be let in, and I have learned that the not-eating is a thing I must choose again each night, the way other women choose prayer. The first of our line did not choose. Her notes survive. I have read them once and will not read them again, and I have not burned them, because a faith without its martyrs is only a habit. The bloom grows thickest at the mouth of the Hollow — the cavity at the foot of the beam through which one enters him. It thickens, I am certain now, in answer to his nearness to himself. To walk through the bloom is to be asked a question by the threshold, and it is always the same question, and the question is will you stay — and one night I will not have my answer ready, and I find I am no longer afraid of that night. That is the measure of how far I have come. I have stopped being afraid of the right things. Of the Beam — The Thing That Was Here First He hangs from a structure older than he is. Mark this well, for it is the part the others overlook. The bone has weathered itself to the beam; the beam has not yielded a single grain to the bone. It is not iron, not stone, not anything I can set a name beside. It does not rust. It takes no heat in summer and no cold in winter. The cross-piece stands against the prevailing wind as though it stood here before the wind had learned which way to blow. Something raised it. Something needed a place to put him. I have written, in three separate years, three separate conclusions as to what that something was, and I have struck out all three — because each frightened me more than my ignorance did. Within the beam there is a hollow, and within the hollow, a stair. It was not built. No mason cut it. It was summoned downward by a need older than masonry — turn upon turn, past every layer the earth keeps secret, to the floor of the world. I have gone as far as the seventh turn. The Watchman goes further. He says little when he returns. I have stopped asking him to say more. Of His Attention Caeleum does not move. And yet nothing near him goes unwatched. The wind crosses the Reach in figures I have copied into the margins and cannot make repeat. Birds will not pass over him. Bees turn at the treeline. The grass beneath the bloom runs blue, as though the ground had taken his colour into its own blood. These things I can record. This I can only confess: to stand beneath the open ribcage is to become the object of a regard so vast and so patient that one's whole life is read in the drawing of a single breath — read, and weighed, and not yet answered. In the first years, I went to study him. I

THE LAST PAGES Of the Men Who Come Men are gathering on the ridgelines. Three nights of fires now, and the fires are arranged, not wandering — someone has taught them to camp in a pattern. They carry iron. They do not pray. They are not pilgrims; pilgrims do not bring crossbows to a god. They want what I have written. I am almost certain of it. Not the gold of the Hollow — gold they could tear from any hillside. They want the knowing. They want to be told what hangs upon the beam, and what lies beneath the water, and why the one bleeds light while the other drinks it down. Someone has sent them to ask, and I do not believe they intend to leave once the asking is done, and I do not believe they intend for me to leave at all. I do not know who sent them. I have a guess. I will not set the guess upon this page. If they take the tome, they will not take the guess along with it. Of the Page I Have Taken There was an entry in this tome upon the matter of Iduun, and the black water that wears its name. It is here no longer. I have torn it out along the binding. You will find the stub of it — a ragged margin where a truth used to live. I did not burn it. To burn a thing is to grant that it can be ended, and I am no longer certain this one can. I keep it close, against my chest, beneath the cloth, where any man who wants it must come through me to reach it. Perhaps that is where it will be found. Perhaps it will be found nowhere at all. If you hold this tome and the page is not with my body, then it has gone where it wished to go, and you should be afraid in a manner I no longer have the room left in me to be. The truth of Iduun is not a thing a lit tower should hold. My tower is full of light. What I wrote of the lake was only ever meant to be read in the dark. — The Keeper, again, and less than before

p A single leaf, torn from the tome along a ragged margin. The hand is smaller and faster than the bound entries. In places the ink has thinned, as though diluted — by water, or by something that was not water. IDUUN I will not give this page a proper heading. A heading is a handle, and I do not wish to be holding this by anything. The lake is not a lake. A lake is fed. A lake feeds something in turn. A river finds it; a river leaves it. Iduun has no river. No stream climbs to it and none escapes. The water does not move, and it is never stagnant. It is full, and it is never filled. I have sounded its depth with every length of line in the tower, knotted end to end, and the line ran out before the bottom did. The lake is deeper than rope. I have come to believe the lake is deeper than the world. It is not the water that watches. The water is only the lid. What lies beneath it is what lies beneath everything. Beneath the stone. Beneath the floor of the world that the spiral stair reaches and cannot pass. Beneath the dark between the high stones at the edge of all maps, where nothing has the decency to fall. It is the same dark. There is only the one dark, and it is patient, and it is everywhere the light forgot to go — and once, only once, leaning over the black water until my own face came back to me too slowly, I understood that it is not empty. Emptiness does not look back. I have seen what it grows where the earth is cut deepest — the blue-black sinew threading the cities that no one built and no one remembers leaving. It listens. It keeps account of what walks above it. The Watchman has gone down to where it sings and will not give me the words, and now I forgive his silence, for I have learned the thing he would not say: that the sinew in the deep and the water in the valley are one thing, wearing the distance between them like a coat. And here is the truth I cannot keep in a lit room. The bloom gives light. The lake takes it. I once held them to be opposites — his blood that shines, her water that swallows. I was a fool in the comfortable way of those not yet frightened. They are not opposites. They are one substance, parted by a wound. His light is what the dark becomes when it is forced to remember it was once a god's. Her dark is what that light becomes when it is allowed, at last, to forget. He hangs above the valley, his ribcage open to the sky. She lies at the centre of the valley, her face turned up. I do not think he was crucified to punish him. I think he was crucified to cover her. I think someone, in a year before there were years, drove him onto that beam not to end him but to hang him over her mouth — a stopper of bone and light, set above the one dark so that it could not look all the way up, and out. And I think the valley endures for a single reason: that he is still looking down, and she is still looking up, and neither has yet been the first to close their eyes.
